content_en:
Tuhfat al-Muhtaj achieved immediate and enduring recognition as the supreme reference of the Hijazi branch of the Shafi'i school. Ibn Hajar's long residence in Mecca, combined with the centrality of that city in the Islamic world, meant that his legal positions spread across a vast geographic range. Scholars from Southeast Asia, East Africa, and South Asia who came to study in Mecca returned home with the Tuhfa as their primary legal reference, and in these regions it became the definitive source for Shafi'i legal determinations.

The work generated a significant secondary literature. The most important of its super-commentaries are the hashiyas of Ibn Qasim al-Abbadi and Ash-Shirwani, which were produced in the generation following Ibn Hajar and which became standard companions to the Tuhfa in madrasa education. These hashiyas added notes, resolved textual difficulties, and extended Ibn Hajar's analysis. The standard printed edition of the Tuhfa includes these hashiyas printed in the margins, so that students reading the text have immediate access to the supplementary commentary.

The comparison with Nihayat al-Muhtaj by ar-Ramli became a productive genre in its own right. Scholars wrote works listing the points of difference between Ibn Hajar and ar-Ramli, helping students navigate the competing authorities. This genre reflects the maturity of the Shafi'i tradition: a school confident enough in its foundations to acknowledge internal diversity and to treat that diversity as a source of intellectual richness rather than a problem requiring resolution.

In contemporary Islamic scholarship, the Tuhfa remains a primary reference for Shafi'i fatwa and for advanced legal education in the Hijaz, Yemen, Indonesia, Malaysia, and East Africa. It is consulted in court proceedings, in formal fatwa institutions, and in personal religious guidance. Ibn Hajar al-Haytami's mark on the Shafi'i tradition is second only to that of an-Nawawi himself. For students and scholars operating in contexts where the Hijazi or Southeast Asian Shafi'i tradition prevails, the Tuhfa is not merely a historical reference but a living authority: its positions continue to be cited in fatwa institutions, taught in traditional madrasas, and consulted by scholars seeking the most authoritative classical statement of Shafi'i law on any given question. This ongoing practical relevance, six centuries after Ibn Hajar's death, testifies to the extraordinary durability of his legal scholarship.
